name: Hydrogen
uses: Ammonia & fertilizer production, rocket fuel, hydrogenation of fats, fuel cells.
fact: Hydrogen makes up about 75% of all normal matter in the universe by mass.

name: Helium
uses: Balloons/airships, cooling MRI magnets (liquid helium), deep-sea breathing mixtures.
fact: Helium was first detected in the Sun's spectrum before it was found on Earth.

name: Lithium
uses: Rechargeable batteries, psychiatric medication (mood stabilizer), ceramics/glass.
fact: Lithium is the lightest metal and can float on oil.

name: Beryllium
uses: Aerospace alloys, X-ray windows, gyroscopes, springs.
fact: Beryllium is transparent to X-rays, so it's used in X-ray tube windows.

name: Boron
uses: Borosilicate glass (Pyrex), detergents, semiconductors, fiberglass.
fact: Boron compounds give fireworks their green color.

name: Carbon
uses: Basis of all organic life, steel-making, graphite in pencils, diamonds, carbon fiber.
fact: Carbon can form more compounds than any other element - over 10 million known.

name: Nitrogen
uses: Fertilizers (via Haber process), food preservation, liquid nitrogen cooling.
fact: Nitrogen gas makes up about 78% of Earth's atmosphere.

name: Oxygen
uses: Respiration, steel manufacturing, water treatment, medical oxygen.
fact: Oxygen is the most abundant element in the Earth's crust by mass.

name: Fluorine
uses: Toothpaste (fluoride), Teflon, refrigerants, uranium enrichment (UF6).
fact: Fluorine is the most reactive and electronegative element on the periodic table.

name: Neon
uses: Neon signs, high-voltage indicators, lasers.
fact: Neon gives off its famous reddish-orange glow when electrified in a tube.

name: Sodium
uses: Table salt (NaCl), street lighting, soap making, heat transfer in reactors.
fact: Sodium is so reactive it must be stored under oil to prevent it reacting with air.

name: Magnesium
uses: Lightweight alloys (aircraft, cars), fireworks, chlorophyll in plants, supplements.
fact: Magnesium burns with a brilliant white light, used in flares and fireworks.

name: Aluminium
uses: Aircraft/vehicle bodies, packaging (foil, cans), electrical wiring, construction.
fact: Aluminium is the most abundant metal in the Earth's crust.

name: Silicon
uses: Semiconductors/microchips, glass, solar cells, silicone polymers.
fact: Nearly all modern computer chips are built on silicon wafers.

name: Phosphorus
uses: Fertilizers, matches, DNA/RNA backbone, detergents.
fact: White phosphorus glows faintly in the dark due to slow oxidation - hence the name (light-bearer).

name: Sulfur
uses: Sulfuric acid production, vulcanization of rubber, fungicides, gunpowder.
fact: Sulfur was historically called 'brimstone' and is referenced throughout ancient texts.

name: Chlorine
uses: Water disinfection, PVC plastic production, bleach, disinfectants.
fact: Chlorine gas was used as a chemical weapon in World War I.

name: Argon
uses: Inert shielding gas for welding, incandescent light bulb filling, wine preservation.
fact: Argon makes up about 1% of Earth's atmosphere, the most abundant noble gas here.

name: Potassium
uses: Fertilizers, essential electrolyte in the body, soap making, glass manufacturing.
fact: Potassium reacts violently with water, producing a lilac flame.

name: Calcium
uses: Bones and teeth, cement production, dietary supplements, metallurgy.
fact: Calcium is the fifth most abundant element in the human body.

name: Scandium
uses: Aerospace alloys, stadium lighting, sporting equipment (aluminium-scandium alloys).
fact: Scandium was predicted by Mendeleev before it was discovered, as 'eka-boron'.

name: Titanium
uses: Aerospace structures, medical implants, white pigment (TiO2), sports equipment.
fact: Titanium is as strong as steel but about 45% lighter.

name: Vanadium
uses: Steel alloying agent, catalysts (sulfuric acid production), batteries.
fact: Vanadium compounds can display several different colors depending on oxidation state.

name: Chromium
uses: Stainless steel, chrome plating, pigments, leather tanning.
fact: Chromium compounds give rubies their red color and emeralds their green.

name: Manganese
uses: Steel manufacturing (deoxidizer), batteries, fertilizers.
fact: Manganese is essential for steel production - almost all steel contains some manganese.

name: Iron
uses: Steel and construction, machinery, hemoglobin in blood, magnets.
fact: Iron is the most common element on Earth by mass, concentrated in the core.

name: Cobalt
uses: Lithium-ion battery cathodes, magnets, blue pigments, superalloys.
fact: Cobalt blue glass has been used as a pigment for over a thousand years.

name: Nickel
uses: Stainless steel, coinage, rechargeable batteries, electroplating.
fact: Nickel gives coins their silvery look and is named after a mischievous sprite in German folklore.

name: Copper
uses: Electrical wiring, plumbing, coinage, antimicrobial surfaces.
fact: Copper is one of the few metals with a natural color other than silver or gray.

name: Zinc
uses: Galvanizing steel, batteries, brass alloy, dietary supplements.
fact: Zinc is essential for immune function and wound healing in the human body.

name: Gallium
uses: LEDs and semiconductors, thermometers, solar panels.
fact: Gallium melts at just above room temperature and can melt in your hand.

name: Germanium
uses: Fiber-optic cables, infrared optics, early transistors, solar cells.
fact: Germanium was predicted by Mendeleev as 'eka-silicon' before it was found.

name: Arsenic
uses: Semiconductors (GaAs), wood preservatives (historically), pesticides.
fact: Arsenic compounds were infamously used as poisons throughout history.

name: Selenium
uses: Photocopiers/photocells, glassmaking, dietary supplement (antioxidant), solar cells.
fact: Selenium's electrical conductivity changes with light exposure, useful in photocells.

name: Bromine
uses: Flame retardants, pesticides, water treatment, pharmaceuticals.
fact: Bromine is the only nonmetallic element that is liquid at room temperature.

name: Krypton
uses: High-performance light bulbs, camera flashes, window insulation.
fact: The meter was once defined using the wavelength of light from krypton-86.

name: Rubidium
uses: Atomic clocks, research, fireworks (purple color).
fact: Rubidium ignites spontaneously in air, even more reactive than sodium or potassium.

name: Strontium
uses: Fireworks (red color), magnets, glow-in-the-dark paints.
fact: Strontium salts produce the brilliant red color seen in many fireworks displays.

name: Yttrium
uses: LED phosphors, superconductors, camera lenses, cancer treatment.
fact: Yttrium is named after the Swedish village of Ytterby, source of four elements.

name: Zirconium
uses: Nuclear reactor cladding, ceramics, jewelry (cubic zirconia).
fact: Zirconium is highly resistant to corrosion, making it ideal for nuclear reactors.

name: Niobium
uses: Superconducting magnets (MRI), steel alloys, jewelry.
fact: Niobium is used in superconducting magnets that power MRI machines.

name: Molybdenum
uses: High-strength steel alloys, catalysts, electrical filaments.
fact: Molybdenum has one of the highest melting points of all elements.

name: Technetium
uses: Medical imaging (radioactive tracer, Tc-99m), industrial radiography.
fact: Technetium was the first element to be artificially produced, hence its name.

name: Ruthenium
uses: Electrical contacts, hard-disk coatings, catalysts, jewelry alloys.
fact: Ruthenium is named after Ruthenia, the Latin name for Russia.

name: Rhodium
uses: Catalytic converters, jewelry plating, electrical contacts.
fact: Rhodium is one of the rarest and most expensive precious metals.

name: Palladium
uses: Catalytic converters, electronics, hydrogen purification, jewelry.
fact: A piece of palladium can absorb up to 900 times its own volume of hydrogen gas.

name: Silver
uses: Jewelry, electrical contacts, photography (historically), antimicrobial coatings.
fact: Silver has the highest electrical and thermal conductivity of any element.

name: Cadmium
uses: Rechargeable NiCd batteries, pigments, electroplating.
fact: Cadmium is toxic and its industrial use has declined due to environmental concerns.

name: Indium
uses: Touchscreens (ITO coatings), solders, semiconductors.
fact: Indium tin oxide is the transparent conductive coating used in most touchscreens.

name: Tin
uses: Solder, tin plating (tin cans), bronze alloy, pewter.
fact: Bending a bar of tin produces an audible 'tin cry' from crystal twinning.

name: Antimony
uses: Flame retardants, lead-acid batteries, semiconductors.
fact: Ancient Egyptians used antimony compounds (kohl) as eye makeup.

name: Tellurium
uses: Alloying agent for steel/copper, solar panels, thermoelectric devices.
fact: Tellurium can give people who ingest it garlic-smelling breath for weeks.

name: Iodine
uses: Antiseptics, iodized salt (thyroid health), X-ray contrast agents.
fact: Iodine sublimes directly from solid to a violet vapor when heated.

name: Xenon
uses: Ion propulsion for spacecraft, high-intensity lamps, anesthesia (research).
fact: Xenon was one of the first noble gases shown to form real chemical compounds.

name: Cesium
uses: Atomic clocks (defines the second), drilling fluids, photoelectric cells.
fact: The international definition of a second is based on cesium-133 atom vibrations.

name: Barium
uses: Medical imaging (barium meals), fireworks (green color), drilling mud.
fact: Barium sulfate is used as a contrast agent for X-ray imaging of the digestive tract.

name: Lanthanum
uses: Camera lenses, catalysts (petroleum cracking), NiMH batteries.
fact: Lanthanum gives its name to the entire lanthanide series of elements.

name: Cerium
uses: Catalytic converters, glass polishing, lighter flints.
fact: Cerium is the most abundant of all the rare-earth elements.

name: Praseodymium
uses: Magnets, welding goggles (glass coloring), aircraft engine alloys.
fact: Praseodymium's name means 'green twin' due to its green oxide compounds.

name: Neodymium
uses: Powerful permanent magnets (headphones, motors), lasers.
fact: Neodymium magnets are among the strongest permanent magnets known.

name: Promethium
uses: Nuclear batteries, luminous paint (historically), research.
fact: Promethium is one of only two radioactive elements that occur with no stable isotopes among rare earths.

name: Samarium
uses: Samarium-cobalt magnets, cancer treatment, nuclear reactor control rods.
fact: Samarium was named after the mineral samarskite, itself named after a Russian mine official.

name: Europium
uses: Red/blue phosphors in TV & LED screens, euro banknote anti-counterfeiting.
fact: Europium is used in euro banknotes as an anti-counterfeiting measure under UV light.

name: Gadolinium
uses: MRI contrast agents, nuclear reactor shielding, data storage.
fact: Gadolinium compounds are injected as contrast agents to improve MRI scans.

name: Terbium
uses: Green phosphors, solid-state devices, fuel cells.
fact: Terbium, like several rare earths, is named after the village of Ytterby, Sweden.

name: Dysprosium
uses: High-strength magnets (with neodymium), data storage, lasers.
fact: Dysprosium's name comes from the Greek for 'hard to get'.

name: Holmium
uses: Medical/industrial lasers, magnets, nuclear control rods.
fact: Holmium has the highest magnetic strength of any naturally occurring element.

name: Erbium
uses: Fiber-optic amplifiers, pink glass/glazes, lasers.
fact: Erbium-doped fiber amplifiers are essential to modern long-distance internet cables.

name: Thulium
uses: Portable X-ray devices, lasers, euro banknote security.
fact: Thulium is one of the rarest and most expensive rare-earth elements.

name: Ytterbium
uses: Atomic clocks, portable X-ray sources, stainless steel strengthening.
fact: Ytterbium is one of four elements named after the village of Ytterby, Sweden.

name: Lutetium
uses: PET scan detectors, petroleum refining catalysts, research.
fact: Lutetium is the hardest and densest of all the lanthanide elements.

name: Hafnium
uses: Nuclear reactor control rods, high-temperature alloys, microprocessors.
fact: Hafnium absorbs neutrons extremely well, ideal for nuclear reactor control rods.

name: Tantalum
uses: Capacitors in smartphones/laptops, surgical implants, alloys.
fact: Tantalum capacitors are used in nearly every smartphone due to their small size.

name: Tungsten
uses: Light bulb filaments, cutting tools, armor-piercing ammunition.
fact: Tungsten has the highest melting point of any metal, at 3,422°C.

name: Rhenium
discovered_by: Walter Noddack, Ida Tacke, Otto Berg
uses: Jet engine superalloys, catalysts, filaments.
fact: Rhenium was one of the last stable elements to be discovered.

name: Osmium
uses: Fountain pen tips, electrical contacts, alloy hardening.
fact: Osmium is the densest naturally occurring element known.

name: Iridium
uses: Spark plugs, crucibles, the historic standard kilogram alloy.
fact: A thin layer of iridium found worldwide is linked to the asteroid impact that ended the dinosaurs.

name: Platinum
uses: Catalytic converters, jewelry, laboratory equipment, cancer drugs.
fact: Platinum is so unreactive that it's used to make some of the most precise lab instruments.

name: Gold
uses: Jewelry, electronics (connectors), dentistry, currency reserves.
fact: All the gold ever mined in human history would fit into roughly three Olympic swimming pools.

name: Mercury
uses: Thermometers (historically), fluorescent lamps, dental amalgams.
fact: Mercury is the only metal that is liquid at standard room temperature.

name: Thallium
uses: Historically used in rat poison/insecticides (now restricted), infrared detectors.
fact: Thallium is extremely toxic and was once nicknamed 'inheritance powder' for its use as an undetectable poison.

name: Lead
uses: Lead-acid batteries, radiation shielding, historically in paints/pipes.
fact: Lead was widely used by the Romans for plumbing - the word 'plumbing' comes from 'plumbum'.

name: Bismuth
uses: Pepto-Bismol (medicine), cosmetics, low-melting alloys, solder.
fact: Bismuth crystals form striking rainbow-colored, staircase-like geometric patterns.

name: Polonium
uses: Anti-static devices, heat sources for spacecraft (historically), research.
fact: Polonium was named by Marie Curie after her native homeland, Poland.

name: Astatine
uses: Experimental cancer radiotherapy (targeted alpha therapy), research.
fact: Astatine is so rare that less than one gram is estimated to exist in Earth's crust at any time.

name: Radon
uses: Historically in cancer radiotherapy, geological/seismic studies.
fact: Radon is a radioactive gas that can accumulate in basements and is a known health hazard.

name: Francium
uses: No significant commercial uses - purely of scientific/research interest.
fact: Francium is so rare and unstable that only about 30 grams may exist on Earth at any moment.

name: Radium
uses: Historically in luminous paints (glow-in-the-dark dials), cancer treatment.
fact: Radium was once used in glow-in-the-dark watch dials, which later proved dangerously radioactive.

name: Actinium
uses: Neutron source in research, targeted cancer therapy (Ac-225).
fact: Actinium glows a faint blue in the dark due to its intense radioactivity.

name: Thorium
uses: Potential nuclear fuel (thorium reactors), gas mantle lighting (historically).
fact: Thorium is being explored as a safer, more abundant alternative nuclear fuel to uranium.

name: Protactinium
uses: Almost no commercial use; purely of research interest.
fact: Protactinium is one of the rarest and most expensive naturally occurring elements.

name: Uranium
uses: Nuclear power reactor fuel, historically nuclear weapons, radiometric dating.
fact: Uranium-238 has a half-life of about 4.5 billion years - roughly the age of the Earth.

name: Neptunium
uses: Research, trace byproduct of nuclear reactors, neutron detection.
fact: Neptunium was the first transuranium element to be discovered, named after the planet Neptune.

name: Plutonium
uses: Nuclear weapons, nuclear reactor fuel, radioisotope thermoelectric generators (spacecraft).
fact: Plutonium powers the radioisotope generators used on deep-space probes like Voyager.

name: Americium
uses: Smoke detectors (ionization type), industrial gauges.
fact: Americium is present in trace amounts in nearly every household smoke detector.

name: Curium
uses: Power source for space probes (historically), scientific research (Mars rover instruments).
fact: Curium was named in honor of Marie and Pierre Curie.

name: Berkelium
uses: Purely research use, precursor for creating heavier synthetic elements.
fact: Berkelium is named after Berkeley, California, where it was first synthesized.

name: Californium
uses: Neutron source for reactor startup, oil well logging, cancer treatment (research).
fact: Californium is so radioactive that a small sample can generate lethal neutron radiation.

name: Einsteinium
uses: Purely scientific research; discovered in debris of a thermonuclear test.
fact: Einsteinium was named in honor of Albert Einstein.

name: Fermium
uses: Purely scientific research.
fact: Fermium was named after physicist Enrico Fermi and, like einsteinium, first found in nuclear test debris.

name: Mendelevium
uses: Purely scientific research.
fact: Mendelevium was named in honor of Dmitri Mendeleev, creator of the periodic table.

name: Nobelium
uses: Purely scientific research.
fact: Nobelium is named after Alfred Nobel, founder of the Nobel Prizes.

name: Lawrencium
uses: Purely scientific research.
fact: Lawrencium is named after Ernest Lawrence, inventor of the cyclotron.

name: Rutherfordium
discovered_by: Joint Institute for Nuclear Research / LBNL
uses: Purely scientific research; extremely short-lived.
fact: Rutherfordium is named after physicist Ernest Rutherford.

name: Dubnium
discovered_by: Joint Institute for Nuclear Research / LBNL
uses: Purely scientific research; extremely short-lived.
fact: Dubnium is named after Dubna, Russia, home of the Joint Institute for Nuclear Research.

name: Seaborgium
uses: Purely scientific research; extremely short-lived.
fact: Seaborgium was named after Glenn Seaborg while he was still alive - a rare honor.

name: Bohrium
uses: Purely scientific research; extremely short-lived.
fact: Bohrium is named after physicist Niels Bohr.

name: Hassium
uses: Purely scientific research; extremely short-lived.
fact: Hassium is named after Hesse, the German state where it was discovered.

name: Meitnerium
uses: Purely scientific research; extremely short-lived.
fact: Meitnerium is named after physicist Lise Meitner, a pioneer of nuclear fission research.

name: Darmstadtium
uses: Purely scientific research; extremely short-lived.
fact: Darmstadtium is named after Darmstadt, Germany, where it was first created.

name: Roentgenium
uses: Purely scientific research; extremely short-lived.
fact: Roentgenium is named after Wilhelm Röntgen, discoverer of X-rays.

name: Copernicium
uses: Purely scientific research; extremely short-lived.
fact: Copernicium is named after astronomer Nicolaus Copernicus.

name: Nihonium
uses: Purely scientific research; extremely short-lived.
fact: Nihonium is named after Japan (Nihon), the country where it was discovered.

name: Flerovium
uses: Purely scientific research; extremely short-lived.
fact: Flerovium is named after physicist Georgy Flyorov.

name: Moscovium
uses: Purely scientific research; extremely short-lived.
fact: Moscovium is named after the Moscow region, home of the Joint Institute for Nuclear Research.

name: Livermorium
uses: Purely scientific research; extremely short-lived.
fact: Livermorium is named after Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory.

name: Tennessine
uses: Purely scientific research; extremely short-lived.
fact: Tennessine is named after the U.S. state of Tennessee, home to several contributing institutions.

name: Oganesson
uses: Purely scientific research; extremely short-lived (heaviest known element).
fact: Oganesson is named after physicist Yuri Oganessian, one of the few elements named after a living person at the time.
